speaker: interviewer
text: Tape  6. The survivor is Samuel Friedman. How many years did you live in Israel?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived, we went to the Beit Bialik camp in Hadera, when we stay a few weeks. In the meantime, my Auntie Runa, Sara and Genia, which were sisters of my-- my mother's sisters, came to visit us. They brought some groups and things. And later on, we went with my auntie, who was living in Netanya. We stayed with her a few days. And we rented a room to stay there and to live-- start living in Netanya. But in the meantime, I had to go to do military service, like everybody else. The new arrivals were not excused. They had to do the military service almost immediately because the army needed new people. And the war was going on with the Arabs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after a few weeks, my uncle advised me that instead of going to the army, I can go to the police force, do the duty. So then I were much better off because I am getting some wages. And  I am at home. I can take care of my wife and and my child, and look for some accommodations, because we were only temporary.

words.text: army, I can go to the police force,

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I accepted this. And he knew the police commissioner. And he accepted me. My Hebrew was bad. But still, I had my matriculation in Hebrew, which is-- gives me some basis. The basis was there. And the rest, I started reading the newspaper quickly. And I started studying a bit more Hebrew, and I could speak quite comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in February, '49, I was accepted in the police force instead of military service. And I was going-- I was working the day or night. But then I was going home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took care of my family. In the meantime, we moved from this place to another place. We-- we started renting a-- half a house. And then I was working only as a patrolman, a patroller. In the meantime, they needed somebody in the investigation bureau. And I was-- was ready to start working. And I started working the investigation bureau of Netanya Police. In the meantime, they-- they looked for candidates for the new-opened police academy in Galilee, in [INAUDIBLE]. And I was accepted to the academy. And I went to the academy. I stayed there nine months, which is a long time. Just can't imagine. We came home every two weeks for-- only for Saturday. And then we-- we worked the day and almost nights. Because at night, we had special night exercises. And the day, in the morning, was physical education. It was jogging and service with arms. And till lunchtime, after lunch, we studied the law-- the law-- the criminal law only, not the civil. The criminal law at this time was the law-- the British law of 1936. And part of this was the Ottoman law for some land problems, and things like this. We studied the law and the procedure-- the criminal procedure and the-- the law of witness.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these are the three we started. The law of witness, this is how to-- how to conduct the witness and how to examine the witnesses. The law of-- the law-- the criminal law  itself, and the law of procedure, criminal procedure. So after nine months, we were quite competent in this. And it was very hard-- very, very hard. And but we got our star on our arms, special star, graduate of the police academy, first   graduate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was accepted in Netanya. I got my stripes immediately. And then I replaced the Chief of Investigation Bureau in Hadera, who got another station. I replaced  him. I got a third stripe. So I was a senior sergeant. I was in charge of the Criminal Investigation Bureau in Hadera. Some prosecution, I did in the courts, in criminal matters only. And well, this was a hard time. But still, it was good. And when I finished my four years, I decided not to go. They wanted to send me further schooling and things. I said, I have had it. No Saturdays, no Sundays, no holidays, night and day, enough. And in the meantime, I took a position as a teacher. But quickly, my cousin who opened the institute for fibers research in Jerusalem with Professor [? Samborsky, ?] under the auspices of David Ben-Gurion, who was then the Prime Minister. And this institute belonged to the office of Prime Minister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, I-- my cousin rang me. And do you want to be the-- my administrator? To run the administration? So I said, well, it's not bad. So I went to Jerusalem, start. And I was the administrator of this institute for the next seven years. I met a lot of people. I met the-- later, the president of Israel, Katchalsky-Katzir. You know the two brothers? Aharon Katzir and Ephraim Katzir?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met a lot of other people from overseas and things. And we were running this until we decided to go to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Israel, after the-- so many years without starting, building an-- without infrastructure, you build nothing. I didn't have my house. In Israel, everybody has his house, or his flat. He buys it, the parents are killing themselves to give some money to the children. And they are killing themselves, working hard. But still, they are getting the-- the-- unit. Like, in Australia, everybody wants his house. In Israel, let's say everybody wants his unit, his flat, for himself. There is no such thing like renting. It didn't exist anyway, at this time. It almost didn't exist, renting. And I couldn't get it anyway because I was just-- I came from the cold-- no family, no anybody. The aunties were very friendly. But they were not supportive financially, or any other-- they could-- they could manage themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said that after 16 years peddling the bike without the chain, we will put the chain on it. And we put the chain on it. Well, we wanted to improve.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then on day, I found myself in '56, with the war in Sinai. I was out of police. So I was called up as a reserve. As a reservist, I was sent to the front with Syria and with Jordan. And I was lying in the trenches, first line, with a machine gun. And behind me, the minefield. The minefield was behind me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was lying there, and the whole night, and waiting for the attack in the morning, which didn't come. And I was thinking, listen, 6 and a 1/2 years war. And now where I am?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am sitting in the trenches with a machine gun. I got  a letter the same morning. Because the post was working somewhat, the military post, from my wife. My son, Jacob, is in hospital with a severe rheumatic fever. And they are-- they have no telephone, no radio, no electricity, no car, no nothing. And then I decided enough is enough. My fighting is over.

speaker: interviewer
text: You understand? This is in the trenches, I decided, this was. I took, immediately, a leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was all over. It was the two or three weeks, all over, so I could take  a leave. And I went by-- by tram-- by hitchhiking to Jerusalem, with my machine gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: I ran strait to the Bikur Cholim Hospital to see my son, with the machine gun. Can you imagine? And I opened the-- I was talking to him and playing with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I opened my-- my machine gun to show him. It was a piece in the FN Belgium, this is an FN Belgium piece, [? where ?] [? you're ?] assembling [? a mice ?] a mouse. So I took it out. And played with him, with the mouse. This I remember. Anyway, this was the end of it, I said. We were living in very hard conditions in a room 23-square meters with three-- with two children, four people, which was absolutely impossible. And we saw that we are going nowhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: '64. So I wrote to Australian consulate. No, in the meantime, I was getting the in Jerusalem. I tried-- I was in London a couple of months, in the meantime, to learn-- to start studying English. Because I wasn't from the English. I was from [? the ?] [? either ?] French or German. And then I was getting I was reading, trying to read, translating with the vocabulary, a dictionary and things. And one of these in '63 was about Australia. Mr. Bolte is welcoming, every year, 600 or 6,000 new migrants. And they are working on the Snowy Mountains Scheme, and things like, blah, blah, blah, you know. I have got this in the book here, the And I read this. And I read through different things. I saw the Bondi Beach. And so I said, listen, this is this. [LAUGHS] I wrote to the consulate. In the meantime, I got some reparations from the Germans for my parents' jewelry store. This was a few thousand pounds. It wasn't a fortune. But this, is I got. I didn't ask-- I asked them not to transfer the money to Israel, because it was very hard to get it out, to keep it in London, in a bank, and to transfer it as-- as soon as I say where. In the meantime, I met a couple who came from Australia. And they-- I ask them, give me a name of a bank in Australia. For example, ANZ Bank is a good one, ANZ. Anyway, so I wrote to the consulate. I got a positive answer. A family, a healthy family with the means-- some means, and we are paying our passage, and we will be no burden to anybody. And they accepted. They wanted to see us. We came the consulate. They sent us to the doctor's. And it was a procedure of three, four, five months. And we got a visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the Snowy Mountain scheme that you mentioned before?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't know? Ah, the Snowy Mountain Scheme, I understand that this was to brings water from one place to another, through the pipes. I was there. I saw it anyway. And this was in the making, in the structure in '63, '64, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the border between the Victoria and New South Wales, in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --Lake Eucumbene-- [? what is it, ?] [? the ?] [? places? ?] I was there, everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mount [? Kozciuszko. ?] Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't go there. I read about this. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I opened a business in Melbourne. Tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my wife studied in [INAUDIBLE], And she made a lot of beautiful pictures, which we were selling in Jerusalem, to the tourists-- themes of Israeli life, camels, and things like this. And my wife is a good designer. And she designed different things. And in the meantime-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --she made a lampshade, one. For somebody, she made a lampshade with elaborate things and designs and things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And our neighbor was the Mayor of Jerusalem. And his wife was very friendly and talkative. And she came, hey, what are you doing? Show me, what you are doing? So he shows her, oy, listen, I must order one for myself-- lampshade. I must have this one. So we made for her, the lampshade. And we went to present her. And she was happy. This is what we-- how we started making lampshade.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to Australia, we were based on the pictures which my wife made. And in Israel, we sold so many and so well. So we decided to make the same thing. But very-- very soon I realized that this is not the-- this is not the product for Australia. You haven't got Jewish tourists from New York, and things, what is a different story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one lady in [INAUDIBLE], a Hungarian-- Jewish-Hungarian lady, she told me-- I ask her, and I showed her what we were doing. She asked me, what else are you doing? My wife knows how to make lampshades.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she said, this is the business. This is the business you should do. So we started making. We made a partner. And we started developing. And because it gives us income, so we continue to develop this. And so we develop this in a nice, income-producing business. My son is running this at the moment. And he is very happy. He is living-- he is making a living. My son-in-law joined us a few years ago. He was very happy. Because he was somewhere unsuccessful in the business. He was in the nursery business. And it was drought, terrible. Nobody was buying. Everything was lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came, joined us. And  he is a very, very important part of our business. And I was wondering that my son, who is not a businessman exactly, he was so good in this, And he was-- he developed this into a real force. We are supplying, now, everybody in Australia, New Zealand, Fiji, and so on. What's the name of the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Skilled Hands Australia Proprietary Limited.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many employees?

speaker: interviewer
text: How active are you in the business still?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am coming four days a week, just to help out, sometimes to replace my son, sometimes, mainly if he wants to go on any holiday. He's not going many times, but still. And it's good I am around to discuss financial problems. And I have-- I have got the depth and the-- and the experience. So sometimes, he is listening to my advice. Sometimes he is not.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he is under no obligation to listen to me. I ask him once, I said, If you want the legal advice, what are you doing? You are going to a lawyer. You may accept advice, you may not accept. But you have to pay him. Now, if you want business advice, you can come to me. It doesn't cost you a penny. And the same story. You may-- you may accept. You may not accept. But this is always good, to hear another-- another-- No? What's your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henrika.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] . Don't tell me to spell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her story, briefly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, her story is that she was sent to Germany, like many Poles, caught in the streets or-- and put on the farms or the factories to work. The condition were  very good. But nobody likes to be taken off the street and to put  in the [? German. ?] But this was the situation then. Some of the Poles were going voluntarily. So it wasn't so bad in the German-- in Germany, to work. But it was unpleasant to be-- not to be with the family. But the correspondence was normal. The post was working. So she was sent, and she was placed at a very rich German family, to take of the children. The children were three, four, five, something like this. And she was 16 or 17. I met her in Friedland, when she was walking somewhere, going somewhere. And she was wearing the P, which every Pole had to wear the P to identify.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I-- I started talking to her. What are you doing? Where are you from? And so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later on, we met many times at the Wintergarten-- Wintergarten, as I mentioned before, where the Poles, we used to meet. Dancing, maybe going out or something. And then she came to me. She told me, my boss, the young boss, who is around 40, has to go to the military service after so many years. This was end of '44. Eventually, they caught him. He has to go. Because he was a big businessman, very well known. So he could maneuver. But it was the end of it. He had to go. Now, he has got a factory to run. The factory, this was a timber mill. The timber  mill was producing different things, tables and munition boxes, and even mines, wooden mines. And he is going to the-- to do military service. And he is looking for somebody, a Pole, who speaks, well, German, who can run the business in the field. Because we have four girls working in the city. They will do the administration. But somebody must be with the-- with the Poles, with the Frenchmen, with the Italian. And his father, who is 73, he can't do it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was chosen. And it was a fight between his factory and the starch factory, who will keep me working. And he won because of his military service and because he was making munition and mines. So he was more important.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I went there. I got a very nice room. They made a room for me. And I was running the factory from inside. Now I had 30 Frenchmen. And they didn't know me. And I-- but I told him who I am. You ask Robert from here, and this from here-- Victor, from here. And the Poles, six or seven Poles, they didn't come to work because there no Germans. Only I was in charge in the factory. So the Poles very soon smelled that I am on their side. And then-- and many times, I came at nine o'clock or half past 10:00, wake them up. Now, listen, the old boss is coming. Come on. Go to-- so they just--

speaker: interviewer
text: So slowly, they-- we build the confidence. Even so much, they-- they killed a cow somewhere, for meat. At three o'clock in the morning, they were knocking on my door bringing me meat and-- and goods, small goods or what they made from this.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were brewing vodka underneath their barracks. But they had it brewing. And they-- they let me in. They had confidence. And so were the Frenchmen. Slowly, they were confident. And so are the Italians, 60 Italians, 50 Italians, 70 Frenchmen. With the Frenchmen I spoke French, and with the Italians, French. And with the-- and it was one only, [? Wachman, ?] the German, with a rifle who was sitting in my office. And he was taking care of the whole camp. Because it was now, as I said, no point of running away. Running away was ridiculous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is your wife Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you tell her that you are a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell-- told her when the Russians came. And she didn't know what I am talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many--

speaker: interviewee
text: So what she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eugenia, my sister, Jacob, my father, and Ruth, my-- not exactly my mother's name. We wanted Rose, but somehow it became Ruth. But  my son, Jacob, married a girl whose name is Rose. So the circle is closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your three children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eugenia was born in Italy. The condition was very hard. But I was working at school. I had a excellent job. And only the passage to Israel was very, very hard. Because we were in these conditions. In Israel, she was a good girl. She is always a good girl. And  she went to school. And she mixed with the other children. She had friends. In Australia, she- she met a man, Australian, Graham. And she decided to marry him. So if she wants, she decided. So she knows, probably, what she does. She went to-- to the [INAUDIBLE] Institute of Technology. They met there. And eventually, she graduate from Monash University, and only a few years ago. And she-- she is a teacher, full-time. Full-time, part-time, as she wants. She is a teacher. And she is very well accepted on the peninsula-- [? Rye, ?] [? Rosebud, ?] everywhere. They want her as a replacement teacher or full teacher, or whatever. She's absolutely magnificent. I-- I-- you know, to do the degree at her age, it was really an effort. She had to do some matriculation and the three years of Monash.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the other two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob is-- went to-- Phillips, as an electronic apprentice, which was the best thing we have ever done. No Harvard, no other universities. And then he went to RMIT. He graduated as an Electronic Technician. And he's very, very useful now in-- with computers-- EDI, everything which has to do with the computers. He is the-- he is the man. He married Shoshanna, which is Israeli girl. They speak Hebrew at home. And the children speak Hebrew. They went to Mount Scopus. We took them out from Mount Scopus, because it's too much-- too much problems. And they are succeeding very, very well. The younger is a pianist, a magnificent piano player. And they are very good children, very intelligent. My big daughter has a son who is 24. He graduated this year from Monash, Industrial Design. He has done well. We didn't expect him to do so. But he did well, fortunately. And the other children are a girl, Michelle, and Andrew. They are at school and doing well at school. My little daughter, Ruth, who was born in Jerusalem-- Jacob was born in Netanya. My little daughter who was born in Jerusalem wrote she has married Peter-- Dr. Peter [? Hartung. ?] He wasn't a doctor. Now he is  a doctor, Peter [? Hartung, ?] who is a high official in the Ministry of-- he is a Director of the arts and in the means of arts and recreation-- sports, arts, and recreation. He's Director of the ministry. And she graduated from Monash-- from Melbourne. Then she made a degree in Monash. Now she's making PhD, doing and finishing her PhD, [? Godsend, ?] for-- in Monash Medical Center. But she is doing this part-time. Because in the meantime, children, and so on. And I still prefer the children to the PhD. The children are very, very nice redheaded boys at school. Very intelligent, computerized completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some of your interests and hobbies today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: My interests? My interest is a bit with the business, involved. Because if I am four days there, so somehow I am interested. I am reading a  lot. I am reading a  lot. I  read, only recently, Biderman's book, about the Lodz Ghetto, just terrible things.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, my story and his story is [? gevalt. ?] And I-- I am reading a lot. I read Patrick White. And-- and now I am about the Babylon, with Mr-- what is his name? [? Forget ?] [? Babylon. ?] You don't know him? He just got a prize. [? I will ?] [? say that. ?] And well, mainly reading, music, business, and family, of course. So-- We have to change the tape.
